play_commands.title: Exploring: Robert Fripp
play_commands.narrative: Robert Fripp is new territory for you — you came here via The Roches, whose self-titled 1979 debut Fripp produced. You already listen to several artists in Fripp's orbit: Brian Eno (3 plays), Peter Gabriel, David Bowie, and David Byrne — all of whom Fripp collaborated with extensively. From his pioneering ambient guitar loops with Eno to leading King Crimson through five decades of reinvention, Fripp sits at a nexus of artists you already love.

suggestions.label: In The Court Of The Crimson King — King Crimson
suggestions.note: The 1969 debut that invented prog rock — essential starting point for King Crimson

suggestions.label: Discipline — King Crimson
suggestions.note: The 1981 new-wave-era KC with Adrian Belew — if you like Talking Heads, start here

suggestions.label: Red — King Crimson
suggestions.note: Heavy, intense 1974 power trio album — widely considered KC's hardest-hitting record

suggestions.label: Evening Star — Robert Fripp & Brian Eno
suggestions.note: Gorgeous ambient collaboration with Eno — the sequel to No Pussyfooting

suggestions.label: Exposure — Robert Fripp
suggestions.note: Fripp's solo debut featuring Peter Gabriel and Daryl Hall — art-rock at its most adventurous

suggestions.label: The First Day — David Sylvian & Robert Fripp
suggestions.note: Dark, atmospheric 1993 collaboration with Japan's David Sylvian

play_commands.title: Exploring: The Roches
play_commands.narrative: The Roches are a folk trio of sisters — Maggie, Terre, and Suzzy Roche — known for their tight harmonies and witty songwriting. Hammond Song, from their acclaimed 1979 debut produced by Robert Fripp, is one of their signature tracks. This is your first recorded listen — a perfect entry point to explore their catalog, which spans from the late '70s through 2007.

suggestions.label: The Roches — The Roches
suggestions.note: Their 1979 debut — home of Hammond Song and the album that started it all.

suggestions.label: Nurds — The Roches
suggestions.note: Their 1980 follow-up, another Robert Fripp production with inventive arrangements.

suggestions.label: Keep On Doing — The Roches
suggestions.note: 1982 album showing the sisters at their most playful and harmonically adventurous.

suggestions.label: Seductive Reasoning — Maggie and Terre Roche
suggestions.note: The pre-Roches 1975 album by Maggie and Terre — raw folk origins before Suzzy joined.

suggestions.label: Moonswept — The Roches
suggestions.note: Their 2007 final studio album — mature songwriting and still gorgeous harmonies.

suggestions.label: The Roches Best (playlist)
suggestions.note: Community-curated playlist of Roches highlights — good overview of their career.

play_commands.title: Siblings
play_commands.room: Dining Room, Kitchen, Living Room, Basement Study

play_commands.title: Lovegaze
play_commands.narrative: Nailah Hunter is the artist Mary Lattimore herself points to as sharing her aesthetic — an LA-based harpist who pairs her instrument with electronics and her own ethereal voice. Lovegaze (2024) is her debut full-length album, weaving harp through ambient textures. Mary was so moved by Nailah's EP Spells that she invited her to play in her front yard during lockdown. This is your gateway into the ambient harp universe that orbits around Mary Lattimore — from Julianna Barwick's layered vocals to Sarah Davachi's organ drones to Alex Somers' Sigur Rós-adjacent soundscapes.

suggestions.label: 🎵 Nailah Hunter — Spells (EP, 2020)
suggestions.note: The EP that blew Mary Lattimore away — harp + electronics + voice

suggestions.label: 🎵 Nailah Hunter — Forest Dark (2022)
suggestions.note: Deeper into the ambient harp forest

suggestions.label: 🎵 Julianna Barwick & Mary Lattimore — Tragic Magic (2026)
suggestions.note: Their joint album — ambient vocals meet harp, inspired by a shared night in Paris

suggestions.label: 🎵 Julianna Barwick — Healing Is A Miracle (2020)
suggestions.note: Her masterpiece — layered vocal loops, featuring Sigur Rós collab

suggestions.label: 🎵 Sarah Davachi — Antiphonals (2021)
suggestions.note: Organ and synth drones — same contemplative space, different instruments

suggestions.label: 🎵 Alex Somers — Siblings (2021)
suggestions.note: Mary's close friend from the Sigur Rós world — music that 'reminds her of breathing'

suggestions.label: 🎵 Mary Lattimore — explore more
suggestions.note: The source of it all — harpist, collaborator, connector

play_commands.title: Exploring: The Stranglers
play_commands.narrative: This is your first recorded listen to The Stranglers — you landed on "Golden Brown" from their 1981 album La Folie. The Stranglers are a British punk/new wave band known for their keyboard-driven sound, with a catalog stretching from 1977's Rattus Norvegicus through the early '90s. La Folie is one of their most acclaimed records, blending post-punk aggression with harpsichord-laced pop on tracks like "Golden Brown."

suggestions.label: Rattus Norvegicus — The Stranglers
suggestions.note: 12-track album from 1977.

suggestions.label: La Folie — The Stranglers
suggestions.note: 17-track album from 1981.

suggestions.label: Black and White — The Stranglers
suggestions.note: 18-track album from 1978.

suggestions.label: No More Heroes — The Stranglers
suggestions.note: 14-track album from 1977.

suggestions.label: BEST OF THE STRANGLERS 🖤
suggestions.note: Community-curated mix of The Stranglers and related artists.

play_commands.title: Kate Bush Radio
play_commands.context: Peter Gabriel connection — Gabriel discovered Kate Bush, handed her demo tape to EMI. They duetted on Don't Give Up.
play_commands.narrative: Kate Bush is your first listen — a bold entry point. Gabriel famously handed her demo tape to EMI when she was just 16, launching one of the most singular careers in pop history. Bush and Gabriel later reunited for "Don't Give Up" on his 1986 album So, a duet that became one of the decade's defining moments. This radio mix spans her full arc, from the teenage theatrics of "Wuthering Heights" through the art-pop masterwork Hounds of Love to her reclusive later experiments.

suggestions.label: Kate Bush — Hounds of Love
suggestions.note: Her 1985 masterpiece — Running Up That Hill, Cloudbusting, and the extraordinary side-long Ninth Wave suite.

suggestions.label: Kate Bush — The Dreaming
suggestions.note: Her most experimental album (1982) — percussion-heavy, wildly theatrical, initially baffled critics but now considered visionary.

suggestions.label: Peter Gabriel — So
suggestions.note: The album that contains their duet Don't Give Up — plus Sledgehammer, In Your Eyes, and Red Rain.

suggestions.label: Joni Mitchell — Court and Spark
suggestions.note: Another fearless female songwriter who refused to repeat herself — your most-played artist after Etta James.

suggestions.label: Kate Bush — Aerial
suggestions.note: Her 2005 comeback after 12 years of silence — meditative, birdsong-laced, utterly unlike anything else.

play_commands.title: Bobby Womack Radio
play_commands.room: Backyard + Basement Study + Dining Room + Kitchen + Living Room + Master Bathroom + Master Bedroom
play_commands.context: User requested Bobby Womack radio to replace current playback
play_commands.narrative: Bobby Womack was one of soul music's most versatile voices — a singer, songwriter, and guitarist who moved from gospel to R&B to funk across five decades. From writing "It's All Over Now" for the Rolling Stones to his own classics like "Across 110th Street," Womack bridged generations of Black American music. This is new territory for your listening — your first Bobby Womack session — but he fits right alongside your favorites Etta James and Nina Simone in that golden era of soul.

suggestions.label: Bobby Womack — The Poet
suggestions.note: His 1981 comeback masterpiece — smoky, intimate soul that revived his career.

suggestions.label: Bobby Womack — Understanding
suggestions.note: Early 70s deep soul, features 'Woman's Gotta Have It' — peak Womack.

suggestions.label: This Is Bobby Womack
suggestions.note: 157-track deep dive if the radio mix leaves you wanting more.

suggestions.label: Etta James — Tell Mama
suggestions.note: Your most-played artist's 1968 Muscle Shoals masterpiece — raw soul that shares Womack's grit.

suggestions.label: Nina Simone — I Put A Spell On You
suggestions.note: 1965 soul-jazz classic from another artist in your rotation — the intensity matches Womack's.

play_commands.title: Stevie Wonder Radio
play_commands.narrative: Stevie Wonder's genius spans five decades of soul, funk, and R&B — from the early Motown prodigy years through the untouchable '70s run of Innervisions, Talking Book, and Songs In The Key Of Life. You've listened to Stevie a few times since early May, and this 215-track radio mix pulls from his full catalog alongside kindred spirits. Perfect backyard music for a late spring evening.

suggestions.label: Stevie Wonder — Songs In The Key Of Life
suggestions.note: His 1976 double-album magnum opus — Sir Duke, Isn't She Lovely, As. One of the greatest albums ever made.

suggestions.label: Stevie Wonder — Innervisions
suggestions.note: The 1973 masterwork that won Album of the Year — Higher Ground, Living for the City. Pure brilliance.

suggestions.label: Stevie Wonder — Talking Book
suggestions.note: The 1972 album that launched his classic period — Superstition, You Are the Sunshine of My Life.

suggestions.label: Nina Simone — Pastel Blues
suggestions.note: Another singular voice who blurred genre lines — you've got 15 plays of Nina. Pastel Blues shares Stevie's emotional depth.

suggestions.label: Etta James — At Last!
suggestions.note: Your most-played artist (69 plays). Etta's soul roots run parallel to Stevie's Motown beginnings.

play_commands.title: Automatic For The People
play_commands.room: Basement Study + Dining Room + Kitchen + Living Room + Master Bathroom + Master Bedroom
play_commands.narrative: Automatic For The People is R.E.M.'s 1992 masterpiece — a meditative, string-laden album that traded the jangle-pop energy of their I.R.S. years for something more orchestral and emotionally vast. Songs like "Everybody Hurts," "Nightswimming," and "Man on the Moon" became generation-defining anthems. This is your first time listening to R.E.M. in this system — a perfect entry point into one of alternative rock's most essential catalogs.

suggestions.label: R.E.M. — Murmur
suggestions.note: Their 1983 debut — the jangly, mysterious college rock record that started it all.

suggestions.label: R.E.M. — Document (R.E.M. No. 5)
suggestions.note: The 1987 breakthrough with 'It's The End Of The World As We Know It' — peak energy R.E.M.

suggestions.label: Steely Dan — Aja
suggestions.note: Meticulous studio craft from another era — you've played Steely Dan 5 times recently.

suggestions.label: Harry Nilsson — Nilsson Schmilsson
suggestions.note: Eclectic 70s pop songwriting with a melancholy edge — from your recent rotation.

suggestions.label: Waxahatchee — Tigers Blood
suggestions.note: Modern indie rock that carries the same confessional songwriting DNA as R.E.M.'s best.

play_commands.title: Sound & Color
play_commands.narrative: Alabama Shakes' sophomore album Sound & Color (2015) is a masterwork of blues-rock experimentation — Brittany Howard's vocals soar over psych-soul arrangements that earned the band four Grammy Awards. This is a new discovery for your library, branching out from your usual jazz and soul rotation. The raw emotional power here connects naturally to the grit of Etta James and the intensity of Nina Simone that you already love.

suggestions.label: Alabama Shakes — Boys & Girls
suggestions.note: Their 2012 debut — rawer, more garage-blues, where it all started with Hold On.

suggestions.label: Brittany Howard — Jaime
suggestions.note: Alabama Shakes' frontwoman solo — genre-bending funk, soul, and psychedelia. New to you.

suggestions.label: Etta James — Tell Mama
suggestions.note: The 1968 Muscle Shoals soul classic — the Alabama blues lineage Alabama Shakes inherited.

suggestions.label: Nina Simone — Pastel Blues
suggestions.note: Same raw vocal intensity and blues foundation, from your listening history.

suggestions.label: Waxahatchee — Saint Cloud
suggestions.note: Katie Crutchfield's Alabama indie roots — warm, intimate Southern songwriting you've been listening to.

play_commands.title: At Last!
play_commands.narrative: Etta James' 1960 debut At Last! is one of the defining albums of American R&B and soul — the title track alone is iconic enough to soundtrack a lifetime of slow dances. You've played Etta 37 times today, so you're clearly deep in a mood. Her raw, powerful voice bridges blues, soul, and jazz in a way that connects directly to Nina Simone (7 plays) and Ella Fitzgerald (6 plays) in your listening history.

suggestions.label: Etta James — Tell Mama
suggestions.note: Her 1968 Muscle Shoals masterpiece — grittier and more soulful than At Last!

suggestions.label: Nina Simone — Pastel Blues
suggestions.note: Same era, same emotional intensity — you've played Nina 7 times recently

suggestions.label: Ella Fitzgerald — Ella and Louis
suggestions.note: The warmth of Ella with Louis Armstrong — a perfect follow-up to Etta's ballads

suggestions.label: Billie Holiday — Lady Sings The Blues
suggestions.note: The original torch singer — new to you but a natural step from Etta's blues roots

suggestions.label: Etta James — Seven Year Itch
suggestions.note: Her 1989 comeback — bluesy and confident, a different side of Etta

play_commands.title: Blue
play_commands.room: Living Room + Kitchen + Dining Room + Backyard + Basement Study + Master Bathroom + Master Bedroom
play_commands.context: User wanted Joni Mitchell classics — Blue or Court and Spark. Chose Blue as the quintessential classic.
play_commands.narrative: Blue is the album that made confessional songwriting an art form — Joni Mitchell alone with her dulcimer, piano, and guitar, laying bare every shade of longing and independence across ten perfect songs. Released in 1971, it sits at the crossroads of folk and something more searching, the same emotional directness you hear in Nina Simone and the harmonic sophistication that would later pull Joni toward the jazz world of Court and Spark and Hejira. You've listened to Joni 12 times this month, and with Bill Evans and Steely Dan in heavy rotation, this is right in your sweet spot — artists who refuse to simplify.

suggestions.label: Joni Mitchell — Court and Spark
suggestions.note: Her 1974 jazz-pop masterpiece — the perfect companion to Blue, with a fuller band sound and songs like Help Me and Free Man in Paris.

suggestions.label: Joni Mitchell — Hejira
suggestions.note: Her 1976 restless road album with Jaco Pastorius on fretless bass — where the folk poet becomes a jazz explorer.

suggestions.label: Joni Mitchell — Both Sides Now
suggestions.note: Her 2000 orchestral reimagining of jazz standards and her own classics — gorgeous for a listener who loves Bill Evans.

suggestions.label: Steely Dan — Aja
suggestions.note: Fellow 70s perfectionists who shared Joni's jazz vocabulary and LA session musicians. You've played them 5 times recently.

suggestions.label: Nina Simone — Pastel Blues
suggestions.note: Another fearless woman at the piano baring her soul — her 1965 album shares Blue's raw emotional honesty. 7 plays in your history.

suggestions.label: Tori Amos — Little Earthquakes (Deluxe)
suggestions.note: Joni's most direct heir — confessional piano songs that channel the same vulnerability as Blue, a generation later.

play_commands.title: At Last!
play_commands.room: Living Room + Dining Room + Kitchen + Master Bathroom
play_commands.context: User asked for Etta James — perfect Sunday soul. Testing new atomic multi-room play.
play_commands.narrative: Etta James' 1960 debut At Last! is the album that defined her — raw, commanding, and achingly tender in the same breath. You've already played her 5 times today, so she's clearly in the air this Sunday morning. With Nina Simone (7 plays) and Ella Fitzgerald (6 plays) also in your recent rotation, you're deep in a lineage of women who bent American music to their will. Shuffled across four rooms for that whole-house soul feeling.

suggestions.label: Etta James — Tell Mama
suggestions.note: Her 1968 Muscle Shoals masterpiece — raw Southern soul at its peak. The title track alone is worth the whole album.

suggestions.label: Etta James — The Dreamer
suggestions.note: Her Grammy-winning 2011 farewell, covering Otis Redding and B.B. King with that unmistakable voice still blazing.

suggestions.label: Etta James — Deep In The Night
suggestions.note: Her 1978 comeback produced by Jerry Wexler — blues-rock Etta at her grittiest, a hidden gem in her catalog.

suggestions.label: Etta James — Blue Gardenia
suggestions.note: Late-career jazz standards from 2001. If you love the tender side of At Last!, this album is pure velvet.

play_commands.title: Kruder & Dorfmeister Radio
play_commands.context: User requested K&D radio in the basement, replacing Rostam Changephobia
play_commands.narrative: A pivot from Rostam's art-pop to the other end of the late-'90s spectrum — Kruder & Dorfmeister's downtempo dub remixes defined an entire genre. Peter Kruder and Richard Dorfmeister took trip-hop, jazz, and dub reggae, ran them through Viennese studio perfectionism, and created something that still sounds like 2 AM at the best bar you've ever been to. Their K&D Sessions double album remains one of the most celebrated remix collections ever pressed. This 200-track radio playlist pulls from their orbit — the artists they remixed (Depeche Mode, Roni Size, Lamb), the scene they shaped (Tosca, Thievery Corporation), and the broader downtempo/trip-hop world they inhabited.

suggestions.label: Tosca — Suzuki
suggestions.note: Richard Dorfmeister's other project — same Viennese dub-lounge DNA but warmer and jazzier, built around live instrumentation and vocal samples

suggestions.label: Thievery Corporation — Mirror Conspiracy
suggestions.note: DC's downtempo ambassadors — bossa nova meets dub meets spy-film soundtrack, peers of K&D in building the late-'90s chill-out canon

suggestions.label: Nightmares on Wax — Smokers Delight
suggestions.note: Warp Records' downtempo masterpiece from 1995 — hip-hop beats, reggae bass, and soul samples that parallel K&D's hazy warmth

play_commands.title: Here's Love
play_commands.context: Evening solo piano — came from Monk → Marcus Roberts thread
play_commands.narrative: Full circle. You started the day with Monk, went through Marcus Roberts, and ended here with the eldest Jones brother. Hank Jones (1918–2010) played with everyone from Charlie Parker to Cannonball Adderley. Where Monk is spiky and Roberts is intense, Jones is warmth itself — 10 plays of this album already. This was the first deep link that actually worked after the controller bug fix.

suggestions.label: Kenny Barron — At The Piano
suggestions.note: Barron inherited Jones's elegance. Both are 'pianist's pianists' — never flashy, always serving the music. Your 39 Bill Evans plays suggest this warmth is your sweet spot.

suggestions.label: Tommy Flanagan — Overseas
suggestions.note: Flanagan, Jones, and Barry Harris were all Detroit pianists — a school of bebop piano that prized taste over technique. Overseas is Flanagan's crown jewel.

suggestions.label: Mulgrew Miller — Solo
suggestions.note: The next generation of the Jones/Flanagan school. Miller died in 2013 — this solo album is his most personal statement.

suggestions.label: Bill Evans — Alone
suggestions.note: Evans's solo album — if Jones is warm sunlight, Evans is candlelight. Same intimacy, different mood. With 39 plays, Evans is your most-listened artist.

suggestions.label: Fred Hersch — Alone at the Vanguard
suggestions.note: Hersch carries the Evans torch into the 21st century. This live solo album at the Vanguard is devastating — especially the ballads.

suggestions.label: Aaron Diehl — Zodiac Suite
suggestions.note: Diehl is the youngest link in this chain — born 1985, classically trained, deeply rooted in the tradition you spent all day exploring.

play_commands.title: Balearic & Chillout
play_commands.narrative: Late afternoon wind-down — Balearic music is the sound of Mediterranean sunsets, Ibiza cafe culture, blissed-out grooves. Your listening pattern shows a clear afternoon arc: intense jazz piano mornings → Steely Dan bridge → ambient/electronic evenings.

suggestions.label: Steely Dan Radio
suggestions.note: Your own playlist — same sophisticated afternoon energy, different temperature. Rock side of the same coin.

play_commands.title: Steely Dan Radio
play_commands.narrative: Your own 121-track Steely Dan Radio playlist — the ultimate yacht rock deep cut collection. Steely Dan sits at an unusual intersection: jazz harmony, rock energy, studio perfectionism. Fagen and Becker hired the best jazz musicians in LA to play rock songs. Your KCRW data even caught "Peg" on Eclectic 24 (10 plays).

suggestions.label: Balearic & Chillout
suggestions.note: Your other mood playlist — similar 'sophisticated afternoon' energy but more electronic/ambient. The Ibiza to Steely Dan's Malibu.

suggestions.label: Bill Evans — Sunday at the Village Vanguard
suggestions.note: Steely Dan worshipped Evans. Fagen's chord voicings come directly from this tradition. Hearing Evans after Dan, you catch the lineage.

suggestions.label: Ahmad Jamal — At The Pershing
suggestions.note: Another Steely Dan influence — Jamal's spacious cool. Listen to 'Poinciana' and hear where 'Do It Again' comes from.

play_commands.title: Night Train
play_commands.narrative: Oscar Peterson is the virtuoso's virtuoso — technical brilliance that never loses its swing. Night Train is his most accessible album, built on blues and standards. After a morning of Monk's angular minimalism, Peterson is the polar opposite: extravagant, generous, overflowing.

suggestions.label: Keith Jarrett — The Köln Concert
suggestions.note: From Peterson's precision to Jarrett's freedom — both are technically staggering but Jarrett lets the music find its own shape.

suggestions.label: Ahmad Jamal — At The Pershing
suggestions.note: Where Peterson fills every space, Jamal leaves space. Miles loved Jamal for this reason. The trio format at its most elegant.

suggestions.label: Erroll Garner — Concert by the Sea
suggestions.note: Garner and Peterson are the two great 'crowd-pleaser' pianists of jazz — both swinging, both joyful, but Garner is looser and more spontaneous.

suggestions.label: Wynton Kelly — Kelly Blue
suggestions.note: The funkiest of the classic jazz pianists. If Peterson is champagne, Kelly is bourbon.

play_commands.title: Changephobia
play_commands.narrative: From deep jazz piano into Rostam's layered indie production — a big mood shift but both share meticulous attention to texture. Rostam (ex-Vampire Weekend) builds songs in washes of synth, harpsichord, and layered vocals. Your Sonos data shows 61 plays of "These Kids We Knew" — he's clearly a go-to.

suggestions.label: Rostam — Half-Light
suggestions.note: His debut solo album. More atmospheric and personal than Changephobia — the harpsichord on 'Wood' is pure Rostam.

suggestions.label: Rostam — American Stories
suggestions.note: Brand new, dropped May 2026. Haven't heard it yet — but Rostam evolving is always worth following.

suggestions.label: Vampire Weekend — Father of the Bride
suggestions.note: Rostam left VW before this album but his DNA is still in their sound. Ezra Koenig went more pastoral — worth hearing what the band sounds like without him.

play_commands.title: Brad Mehldau — After Bach
play_commands.narrative: From jazz piano to the classical-jazz boundary. Mehldau alternates Bach pieces with his own compositions inspired by them — a conversation across 300 years. This is where jazz piano meets its deepest roots.

suggestions.label: Aaron Diehl — Zodiac Suite
suggestions.note: Diehl reviving Mary Lou Williams' 1945 suite with a full orchestra. Classical precision meets jazz soul — you already have 15 plays of his 'Prologue'.

suggestions.label: Aaron Diehl — The Bespoke Man's Narrative
suggestions.note: Diehl's debut — elegant, classically informed jazz. He studied with both jazz and classical masters.

suggestions.label: Keith Jarrett — The Köln Concert
suggestions.note: If Mehldau bridges Bach and jazz deliberately, Jarrett does it intuitively — 66 minutes of pure improvisation that sounds composed.

suggestions.label: Fred Hersch — Alone at the Vanguard
suggestions.note: Hersch brings the same classical sensitivity as Mehldau but in a live solo setting. Devastating ballad interpretations.

suggestions.label: Bill Evans — Sunday at the Village Vanguard
suggestions.note: The album that changed jazz piano forever. Evans' trio invented a new kind of group interplay — and your 39 Evans plays say this is home territory.

play_commands.title: As Serenity Approaches
play_commands.context: Solo piano deep dive — followed Monk
play_commands.narrative: Straight from Monk into Marcus Roberts. Roberts studied with Monk's collaborators and carries that lineage forward — but he also brings deep roots in gospel and stride piano. Blind since age 5, he plays with a physical intensity you can hear in every note. This was the second stop on your solo piano morning.

suggestions.label: Marcus Roberts — The Joy of Joplin
suggestions.note: Roberts reimagining Scott Joplin rags — connects ragtime to modern jazz piano. The stride tradition Monk grew out of.

suggestions.label: Marcus Roberts — Gershwin For Lovers
suggestions.note: Roberts on Gershwin standards — lush, romantic, still distinctly his voice.

suggestions.label: Kenny Barron — At The Piano
suggestions.note: Barron is the quiet master — never flashy, always perfect. Your listening history shows 39 Bill Evans plays; Barron is Evans' spiritual successor.

suggestions.label: Mulgrew Miller — Solo
suggestions.note: Miller died too young. This solo album captures his warmth and harmonic sophistication — a bridge between Evans and modern jazz piano.

suggestions.label: Hank Jones — Here's Love
suggestions.note: You ended up here later — the eldest Jones brother, elegant and swinging. 10 plays already. Full circle from Monk.

suggestions.label: Erroll Garner — Concert by the Sea
suggestions.note: Garner couldn't read music but played with irresistible joy. This 1955 live album is pure happiness. The opposite of Monk's angularity — but equally authentic.

suggestions.label: Wynton Kelly — Kelly Blue
suggestions.note: Kelly played on Kind of Blue, then Miles replaced him with Evans. This is Kelly's own statement — bluesy, swinging, soulful.

play_commands.title: Alone in San Francisco
play_commands.narrative: Your first Tasklet play — and it started a whole thread. Monk recorded this in 1959 at a San Francisco studio, completely solo, no rhythm section to hide behind. Every quirky hesitation and dissonant cluster is just him and the piano. This session kicked off a deep dive into the solo piano tradition that ran through Marcus Roberts, Hank Jones, and beyond.

suggestions.label: Bill Evans — Alone
suggestions.note: Evans did his own solo album a decade later — more lyrical and introspective than Monk, but the same courage of playing completely exposed.

suggestions.label: Bill Evans — Alone (Again)
suggestions.note: The sequel, recorded in 1975. Deeper, more meditative. Evans was in a different place by then.

suggestions.label: Marcus Roberts — Alone With Three Giants
suggestions.note: Roberts explicitly carries Monk's torch — this album tackles Monk, Ellington, and Jelly Roll Morton solo. You went here next.

suggestions.label: Fred Hersch — Alone at the Vanguard
suggestions.note: Live solo piano at the Village Vanguard. Hersch is one of the few pianists trusted to do a solo week there. Impressionistic, unhurried.

suggestions.label: Keith Jarrett — The Köln Concert
suggestions.note: The most famous solo piano recording in jazz — completely improvised, one unbroken flow. If Monk is architecture, Jarrett is stream of consciousness.

suggestions.label: Duke Ellington — Money Jungle
suggestions.note: Not solo, but Ellington with Mingus and Max Roach — three giants colliding. Raw, combative, nothing like his orchestral work.

suggestions.label: Tommy Flanagan — Overseas
suggestions.note: Flanagan was Monk's opposite — elegant, understated, bebop-fluent. This 1957 session in Stockholm is his masterpiece.

suggestions.label: Ahmad Jamal — At The Pershing
suggestions.note: Miles Davis said 'I want my band to sound like Ahmad Jamal.' This live trio album defined a whole school of spacious, dynamic jazz piano.
